items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.correct: Removing material from a solid block to reveal the form (e.g., carving stone).
items.why: Students confuse 'subtractive' with arithmetic. In sculpture, additive = building up (clay, welding); subtractive = taking away (carving). Michelangelo described 'freeing' figures from the marble - quintessential subtractive thinking.

items.correct: Art that does not depict any recognisable object - exploring form, colour, and line directly.
items.misconception: Art that looks 'a bit like' something but not exactly.
items.random_wrong: Art that doesn't represent any country.
items.why: Students soften the term to 'stylised'. Non-representational means NO reference to objects at all - pure visual exploration (Rothko, Mondrian), distinct from semi-abstract work that simplifies recognisable forms.

items.q: The Benin Bronzes were looted in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1897. “1797” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Sarr-Savoy Report was published in:
items.why: It's 2018. “2008” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: From the time the Parthenon Marbles were removed (1801) to 2026, how many years have passed?
items.why: Exact answer: 225. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
